steps.title: Compare how concentrated each distribution is around its mean
steps.content:
Group A (mean 2.1): Frequencies 6–3–2–3–6 spread across weights 0 through 4.

Most objects are far from the mean at weights 0 and 4.

Group B (mean 11.1): Frequencies 2–3–10–3–2 across weights 9 through 13. 10 out of 20 objects sit exactly at weight 11 (the mean), with very few objects in the tails.

steps.title: Recognize that concentration at the mean reduces standard deviation
steps.content:
Standard deviation measures how spread out data is from the mean.

To compare, note that both groups span the same range of deviations from their respective means: −2.1, −1.1, −0.1, 0.9, 1.9.

However, Group B places 10 objects at deviation −0.1 (nearly zero), while Group A has only 2 objects there.

Conversely, Group A places 6 objects at deviation −2.1 and another 6 at deviation +1.9, while Group B places only 2 at each.

Since larger squared deviations contribute more to the sum, Group A—with more frequency at larger deviations—has a larger variance and standard deviation.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
